title: Elon Musk's Doge Project and the Automation of Government
content:
Elon Musk's 'Doge' initiative introduced a sweeping transformation in the federal government. Gaining the authority to email all federal employees, the Doge team made the same offer Musk had previously used at Twitter: quit with paid leave or face likely termination. This pattern recurred across the government as Musk announced his intention to delete entire agencies. He compared this to removing the roots of a weed so it wouldn't grow back. From the start, gaining access to databases and digital infrastructure was a top priority.

Establishing centralized command-and-control positions, Musk's surrogates rolled out a playbook focused on deletion, automation, and integration. The logic of deletion was most evident in zero-based budgeting (ZBB), a method that forces departments to justify every expense anew. Although long dismissed as unworkable, Silicon Valley firms argued that large language models and AI accounting tools had finally made ZBB feasible. Consequently, budgets could be rebuilt automatically by bots without terribly time-intensive manual analysis. According to reports, Musk quickly captured key computer systems to create a 'delete' button against any agency by cutting its funding.

However, zero-based budgeting rarely succeeds in actually cutting costs. Its real effect in Musk's hands was the unprecedented concentration of power. His approach assumed that all expenditures were waste and that anything deemed 'bad data' could simply be deleted. As media researchers noted, Doge sought to automate not just paperwork but democratic decision-making itself. Ultimately, efficiency became the alibi for centralizing control and reshaping the federal bureaucracy.
